execution_details.stdout:
# Example configuration file for AIDE.

@@define DBDIR /var/lib/aide
@@define LOGDIR /var/log/aide

# The location of the database to be read.
database=file:@@{DBDIR}/aide.db.gz

# The location of the database to be written.
#database_out=sql:host:port:database:login_name:passwd:table
#database_out=file:aide.db.new
database_out=file:@@{DBDIR}/aide.db.new.gz

# Whether to gzip the output to database
gzip_dbout=yes

# Default.
verbose=5

report_url=file:@@{LOGDIR}/aide.log
report_url=stdout
#report_url=stderr
#NOT IMPLEMENTED report_url=mailto:root@foo.com
#NOT IMPLEMENTED report_url=syslog:LOG_AUTH

# These are the default rules.
#
#p:      permissions
#i:      inode:
#n:      number of links
#u:      user
#g:      group
#s:      size
#b:      block count
#m:      mtime
#a:      atime
#c:      ctime
#S:      check for growing size
#acl:           Access Control Lists
#selinux        SELinux security context
#xattrs:        Extended file attributes
#md5:    md5 checksum
#sha1:   sha1 checksum
#sha256:        sha256 checksum
#sha512:        sha512 checksum
#rmd160: rmd160 checksum
#tiger:  tiger checksum

#haval:  haval checksum (MHASH only)
#gost:   gost checksum (MHASH only)
#crc32:  crc32 checksum (MHASH only)
#whirlpool:     whirlpool checksum (MHASH only)

#R:             p+i+n+u+g+s+m+c+acl+selinux+xattrs+md5
#L:             p+i+n+u+g+acl+selinux+xattrs
#E:             Empty group
#>:             Growing logfile p+u+g+i+n+S+acl+selinux+xattrs

# You can create custom rules like this.
# With MHASH...
# ALLXTRAHASHES = sha1+rmd160+sha256+sha512+whirlpool+tiger+haval+gost+crc32
ALLXTRAHASHES = sha1+rmd160+sha256+sha512+tiger
# Everything but access time (Ie. all changes)
EVERYTHING = R+ALLXTRAHASHES

# Sane
# NORMAL = R+sha512
NORMAL = p+i+n+u+g+s+m+c+acl+selinux+xattrs+sha512

# For directories, don't bother doing hashes
DIR = p+i+n+u+g+acl+selinux+xattrs

# Access control only
PERMS = p+u+g+acl+selinux+xattrs

# Logfile are special, in that they often change
LOG = p+u+g+n+S+acl+selinux+xattrs

# Content + file type.
CONTENT = sha512+ftype

# Extended content + file type + access.
CONTENT_EX = sha512+ftype+p+u+g+n+acl+selinux+xattrs

# Some files get updated automatically, so the inode/ctime/mtime change
# but we want to know when the data inside them changes
DATAONLY =  p+n+u+g+s+acl+selinux+xattrs+sha512

# Next decide what directories/files you want in the database.

/boot       CONTENT_EX
/opt        CONTENT

# Admins dot files constantly change, just check perms
/root/\..* PERMS
!/root/.xauth*
# Otherwise get all of /root.
/root   CONTENT_EX

# These are too volatile
!/usr/src
!/usr/tmp

# Otherwise get all of /usr.
/usr    CONTENT_EX

# trusted databases
/etc/hosts$      CONTENT_EX
/etc/host.conf$  CONTENT_EX
/etc/hostname$   CONTENT_EX
/etc/issue$      CONTENT_EX
/etc/issue.net$  CONTENT_EX
/etc/protocols$  CONTENT_EX
/etc/services$   CONTENT_EX
/etc/localtime$  CONTENT_EX
/etc/alternatives CONTENT_EX
/etc/sysconfig   CONTENT_EX
/etc/mime.types$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/terminfo    CONTENT_EX
/etc/exports$    CONTENT_EX
/etc/fstab$      CONTENT_EX
/etc/passwd$     CONTENT_EX
/etc/group$      CONTENT_EX
/etc/gshadow$    CONTENT_EX
/etc/shadow$     CONTENT_EX
/etc/subgid$     CONTENT_EX
/etc/subuid$     CONTENT_EX
/etc/security/opasswd$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/skel        CONTENT_EX
/etc/sssd        CONTENT_EX
/etc/machine-id$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/swid        CONTENT_EX
/etc/system-release-cpe$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/shells$     CONTENT_EX
/etc/tmux.conf$  CONTENT_EX
/etc/xattr.conf$ CONTENT_EX

# networking
/etc/firewalld      CONTENT_EX
!/etc/NetworkManager/system-connections
/etc/NetworkManager CONTENT_EX
/etc/networks$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/dhcp CONTENT_EX
/etc/wpa_supplicant CONTENT_EX
/etc/resolv.conf$ DATAONLY
/etc/nscd.conf$ CONTENT_EX

# logins and accounts
/etc/login.defs$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/libuser.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/var/log/faillog$ PERMS
/var/log/lastlog$ PERMS
/var/run/faillock PERMS
/etc/pam.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/security CONTENT_EX
/etc/securetty$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/polkit-1 CONTENT_EX
/etc/sudo.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/sudoers$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/sudoers.d CONTENT_EX

# Shell/X startup files
/etc/profile$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/profile.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/bashrc$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/bash_completion.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/zprofile$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/zshrc$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/zlogin$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/zlogout$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/X11 CONTENT_EX

# Pkg manager
/etc/dnf CONTENT_EX
/etc/yum.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/yum CONTENT_EX
/etc/yum.repos.d CONTENT_EX

# This gets new/removes-old filenames daily
!/var/log/sa
# As we are checking it, we've truncated yesterdays size to zero.
!/var/log/aide.log

# auditing
# AIDE produces an audit record, so this becomes perpetual motion.
/var/log/audit PERMS
/etc/audit CONTENT_EX
/etc/libaudit.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/aide.conf$  CONTENT_EX

# System logs
/etc/rsyslog.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/rsyslog.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/logrotate.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/logrotate.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/systemd/journald.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/var/log LOG+ANF+ARF
/var/run/utmp LOG

# secrets
/etc/pkcs11 CONTENT_EX
/etc/pki CONTENT_EX
/etc/crypto-policies CONTENT_EX
/etc/certmonger CONTENT_EX
/var/lib/systemd/random-seed$ PERMS

# init system
/etc/systemd CONTENT_EX
/etc/rc.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/tmpfiles.d CONTENT_EX

# boot config
/etc/default CONTENT_EX
/etc/grub.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/dracut.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/dracut.conf.d CONTENT_EX

# glibc linker
/etc/ld.so.cache$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/ld.so.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/ld.so.conf.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/ld.so.preload$ CONTENT_EX

# kernel config
/etc/sysctl.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/sysctl.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/modprobe.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/modules-load.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/depmod.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/udev CONTENT_EX
/etc/crypttab$ CONTENT_EX

#### Daemons ####

# cron jobs
/etc/at.allow$ CONTENT
/etc/at.deny$ CONTENT
/etc/anacrontab$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/cron.allow$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/cron.deny$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/cron.d CONTENT_EX
/etc/cron.daily CONTENT_EX
/etc/cron.hourly CONTENT_EX
/etc/cron.monthly CONTENT_EX
/etc/cron.weekly CONTENT_EX
/etc/crontab$ CONTENT_EX
/var/spool/cron/root CONTENT

# time keeping
/etc/chrony.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/chrony.keys$ CONTENT_EX

# mail
/etc/aliases$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/aliases.db$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/postfix CONTENT_EX

# ssh
/etc/ssh/sshd_config$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/ssh/ssh_config$ CONTENT_EX

# stunnel
/etc/stunnel CONTENT_EX

# printing
/etc/cups CONTENT_EX
/etc/cupshelpers CONTENT_EX
/etc/avahi CONTENT_EX

# web server
/etc/httpd CONTENT_EX

# dns
/etc/named CONTENT_EX
/etc/named.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/named.iscdlv.key$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/named.rfc1912.zones$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/named.root.key$ CONTENT_EX

# xinetd
/etc/xinetd.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/xinetd.d CONTENT_EX

# IPsec
/etc/ipsec.conf$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/ipsec.secrets$ CONTENT_EX
/etc/ipsec.d CONTENT_EX

# USB guard
/etc/usbguard CONTENT_EX

# Ignore some files
!/boot/grub2/grubenv$
!/etc/mtab$
!/etc/.*~

# Now everything else
/etc    PERMS

# With AIDE's default verbosity level of 5, these would give lots of
# warnings upon tree traversal. It might change with future version.
#
#=/lost\+found    DIR
#=/home           DIR

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to remediating AIDE use FIPS hashes. The plan: ensure /etc/aide.conf includes sha512 in rules. Remove disallowed hash algorithms. So we propose to read /etc/aide.conf, modify lines as needed.

We need to produce a plan description to review_plan. Must call review_plan with plan_description.

We must abide by tool usage: We need to produce plan in first response. Then call review_plan. Then if approved, apply. Let's design plan.

Plan: Steps:

1. Read /etc/aide.conf to inspect current contents. Find lines starting with rule names: maybe "NORMAL =" etc. Guarantee that sha512 is present, and forbidden hashes are not included. We'll also ensure comments starting with # are commented.

The plan: use sed to adjust each rule. For each rule group (any line defined earlier). We'll parse groups: lines like "NORMAL = ...". We'll modify each.

We need to not add duplicate values. Use sed to add "+sha512" if not present. Remove any forbidden hashed via sub patterns.

Implementation: Use sed -i -e 's/^NORMAL\s*=\s*\([^#]*\)$/NORMAL = \1/ ' - but we will just match rule lines.

Alternatively we can run a script that processes file line-by-line and writes new file. But we need to use only one command at a time; using sed might be easier.

Potential approach: Use the remediation script provided: a shell script that loops groups and modifies config. Since we are supposed to use run_cmd only one command at a time, but we can run multiple run_cmd in sequence. We can just run the script lines sequentially? But each run_cmd command must be a single shell command, not multiple. However we can create a temporary script file and run it with sh. But that would be multiple commands: write_file + run_cmd. That could be acceptable.

But the review expects plan. Let's propose: we will write a small script to /tmp/aide_fips_fix.sh containing commands that loop through groups, modify config. Then run it. Alternatively we can modify file inline by sed for each rule. The rule names might be many; but we can just process for all rule patterns: e.g. "sh+"? Actually we need to find a line per group or if not present, add a new rule at end of file.

Simplify: We will locate lines that define rules like "NORMAL = ..." etc. We'll replace the rule to include sha512 and remove forbidden ones.

We can use sed command for each line: sed -i -r 's/(^([A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=\s*))([^#]*)(#.*)?$/{...}'.

But writing such sed in single command may be complex.

Alternatively use the provided script logic but use sh -c '...' for each step. Use run_cmd to call the script. Since each run_cmd is single command, we can write:

run_cmd: content: sh -c '...'.

But we can only use run_cmd as one command: e.g. run_cmd {... command: "sh -c '<script>'"}. That is fine.

But we might prefer to modify the config directly.

Another approach: Use 'grep -v -E' to list each prohibited algorithm.

Simpler: At top of plan: we will run 'grep' to find lines, modify them.

But we can provide plan: "We will add sha512 to any rules that do not have it, and remove the forbidden hashes (sha1 rmd160 sha256 whirlpool tiger haval gost crc32). We'll do this by editing /etc/aide.conf directly: first, read it, then run sed to insert+sha512 where missing and remove forbidden tokens."

We'll propose to run the shell commands:

First read file: read_file /etc/aide.conf.

Then run_cmd: sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/ s/$/ +sha512/' folder? Actually we need to add only if not present.

Using sed conditional: s/^\(.*\)sha512\>/\1/ ; but that's removal.

To add, we can use placeholder replacement: sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/ { /sha512/! s/=\s*\([^#]*\)/= \1 +sha512/ }'.

And to remove forbidden: we can iterate through list with sed -e 's/sha1//g; s/rmd160//g; ...', but we want to keep the rest. So we do sed to remove patterns.

But may create double spaces; we can run a cleanup.

So plan: 
- Use sed to add +sha512 if missing.
- Use sed to remove any forbidden algorithms in config lines.
- Ensure not duplicate.

- Use sed to remove extra spaces.

Implement via a series of sed commands with run_cmd.

Alternatively we can use the script logic: create groups via grep lines of rule names then for loop.

But we cannot run multiple commands. We can run script with sh -c 'group script'.

But the script itself may contain line breaks; we must place them in single command string. That is acceptable.

Let's propose to create a script variable in the command string.

E.g.:

run_cmd: { "command": "sh -c 'aide_conf=/etc/aide.conf; forbidden_hashes=(sha1 rmd160 sha256 whirlpool tiger haval gost crc32); ;'"}.

But that includes quotes. That's complicated.

Better: use sed editing directly.

Let's craft sed command(s).

First to add sha512:

run_cmd: {command: "sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\\s*=\\s*/{ /sha512/! s/=$/ +sha512/ }' /etc/aide.conf"}

But we also need to handle lines that end with hash options separated by spaces. Eg: "NORMAL = FIPSR+sha512". That line may not have spaces after. Our sed: For line with rule, we will append " +sha512" if 'sha512' not present.

So the pattern: for each line starting with uppercase and ending with '='? Actually rule lines: e.g., "NORMAL = FIPSR+sha512". Add at end of the line after existing options.

But we might have lines where after '=' some spaces and hash options separated by '+', maybe with other tokens. Let's consider typical AIDE config: "NORMAL = FIPSR+sha512". So the line may be 'NORMAL = FIPSR+sha512' or 'NORMAL = FIPSR'. We'll add sha512 after plus if not existing.

We can run: sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=\s*[^#]*$/ { /sha512/! s/^\([A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=\s*\)[^#]*$/\1sha512/ }'.

But we need to keep the existing config unchanged except for adding sha512.

Given complexity, maybe easier to run script that loops groups and echo new config lines into new file, then replace file. Let's do:

Write script file /tmp/aide-fips-fix.sh:

#!/bin/sh
aide_conf="/etc/aide.conf"
temp="/tmp/aide.conf.tmp"
# copy header lines not rule lines
grep -v '^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=' "$aide_conf" > "$temp"
# parse rules
for group in $(LC_ALL=C grep "^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*" "$aide_conf" | cut -f1 -d ' ' | tr -d ' ' | sort -u); do
    config=$(grep "^$group\s*=" "$aide_conf" | cut -f2 -d '=' | tr -d ' ')
    # add sha512
    if ! echo "$config" | grep -q sha512; then
        config="$config+sha512"
    fi
    # remove forbidden
    for hash in sha1 rmd160 sha256 whirlpool tiger haval gost crc32; do
        config=$(echo "$config" | sed "s/%s//g" "$hash")
    done
    echo "$group = $config"
done >> "$temp"
mv "$temp" "$aide_conf"

But this script uses multiple commands. Running it via 'sh -c "$(cat /tmp/... )"' is complex.

Alternatives: Use sed partial replacement with pattern replacement to remove forbidden: sed -i -e 's/sha1//g' -e 's/rmd160//g' etc on rule lines.

But need to ensure not to remove part of other tokens accidentally (like "FIPSdump"). But the forbidden list are simple words. Use pattern with word boundaries.

But sed not support \b? Might use 's/\bsha1\b//g'.

On Linux sed, may use '\b' only in GNU sed. We are on Rocky, uses GNU sed. So okay.

So steps:

1. Add sha512 if missing:

sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/ { /sha512/! s/=\s*\([^#]*\)$/= \1 +sha512/ }' /etc/aide.conf

But we need to ensure we match end of line, may include spaces. This will add " +sha512" separated by space.

2. Remove forbidden hashes: For each forbidden, run sed to delete them:

for hash in sha1 rmd160 sha256 whirlpool tiger haval gost crc32; do
    sed -i -e 's/\b'"$hash"'\b//g' /etc/aide.conf
done

We'll run run_cmd for each sed command.

Also, ensure we trim any extra spaces and plus signs.

But the rule might become "NORMAL = FIPSR +sha512 " with extra spaces; we can run a clean-up command: replace multiple spaces or plus signs accordingly.

Finally, we can run sed to collapse + separator duplication.

But maybe not necessary if path correct.

Look at typical rule: "NORMAL = FIPSR+sha512". If we add "+sha512" again, duplicates? Our sed only checks if sha512 not there. So safe.

Remove forbidden from entire rule line; we must preserve preceding options and plus signs.

Potential issue: If the flag appears inside other strings (like 'sha1' inside 'FIPSR+sha123'), but not likely. Good.

Edge: If rule lines end with '# comment', we need to preserve comment. Our sed will modify only part before comment.

So pipeline for comment: use 's/#.*/#&/'? But our pattern works before comment: We matched up to end-of-line; but if comment after, we need to adapt.

Our sed: 's/=\s*\([^#]*\)$/= \1 +sha512/' will stop at comment line end? Actually for lines with comment, the $ will be comment. The pattern [^#]* stops before comment; but then we have '=$' which doesn't match because there is a comment char before end. So we need to match both [^#]* followed by optional comment.

Simplify: We can use two-step approach: for all lines that define rules, trim any comment part using separate sed later. We'll add sha512 to the part before comment then reappend comment if existed.

But we can circumvent by using sed to preserve comment: 's/^\(.*=\s*\)[^#]*\(\s*#.*\)\?$/\1\r\2/'? Too complex.

Alternative: use simpler approach: For each rule line, let sed run on part before comment; we can capture comment.

Use pattern: 's/^\(.*=\s*\)\([^#]*\)\(\s*#.*\)\?$/\1\2 +sha512\3/' Let me draft.

But easier: We can run script to remove comment, process, then add comment back. But script approach may be heavier.

Let's evaluate typical config: Usually rules are on separate lines without comments after. We can assume no comment at end. Risk low.

For simplicity, we will assume not comment.

Better: Use grep to extract rule lines, perform modifications, then replace them back.

But given limited time, we can deliver plan as we think.

Let's propose plan:

- Plan: use sed to add sha512 to any rule lacking it (normal rules and others).
- Use sed to remove forbidden hashes from rule lines.
- If missing sha512 entirely (rule lines already include), ensure only one.

To confirm, we will pre-read file to check lines.

But we don't need to produce final config; just a plan.

Ok, deliver plan.

We need to implement the plan. Steps: Ensure ensure AIDE installed? The plan didn't mention installation, but maybe it's safe to assume installed. But let's check. We can just run dnf install -y aide. But plan didn't mention. But risk minimal. We can implement installation? The rule might only apply if AIDE is installed. We could check if usa. We need to abide by instructions: Use read_file before modifying. Use sed -i to modify lines: no duplicate lines. Ensure we don't affect comments. Use read_file to inspect current file. Let's read /etc/aide.conf. Then we need to run sed commands.

Need to add sha512 to rules that don't have it. The sed expression from review improvements: `s/^\([A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=\s*\)[^#]*$/\1\1+sha512/` seems odd. Actually to append +sha512 to value part. Safer: `sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/ { /sha512/! s/=\(.*\)/=\1+sha512/ }' /etc/aide.conf`. But careful about spaces.

Plan: Use one command: `sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/{/sha512/! s/=\([^#]*\)/=\1+sha512/}' /etc/aide.conf` ?

So search lines starting with upper-case word, not commented. For lines with '=' that don't contain `sha512`, append `+sha512`. Use sed: `/^[A-Z]\w*\s*=/ { /sha512/! s/=\(.*\)$/=\1+sha512/ }`. The plan with cleaning steps: `sed -i -e 's/\++/+/g' -e 's/ +$//' -e 's/^\s*//' -e 's/\s*$//'`. But we should be cautious.

Ok. Let's implement.

We also need to ensure we don't modify comments. Our sed addresses restrict to rule lines.

Let's propose commands:

- Command 1: `dnf install -y aide`
- Command 2: `sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/{/sha512/! s/=\(.*\)$/=\1+sha512/}' /etc/aide.conf`
- Command 3: repeat for each hash: we can combine multiple in one sed: `sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/{s/\\b\(sha1\|rmd160\|sha256\|whirlpool\|tiger\|haval\|gost\|crc32\)\\b//g}' /etc/aide.conf`

But need to escape properly.

Simpler to run multiple separate run_cmd. But efficiency not needed. We'll run each hash removal step individually. 8 loops; but allowed? Could exceed limit. But can combine into one sed with OR.

Sed pattern: `s/\b\(sha1\|rmd160\|sha256\|whirlpool\|tiger\|haval\|gost\|crc32\)\b//g`.

But `\b` need to be word boundary: works in GNU sed (maybe). Let's just use `\<` or `\b`. Use `\<` for word start, `\>` for end: `s/\<\(sha1\|rmd160\|sha256\|whirlpool\|tiger\|haval\|gost\|crc32\)\>/\1/g`??? Actually want to remove. So `s/\b\(sha1\|rmd160\|sha256\|whirlpool\|tiger\|haval\|gost\|crc32\)\b//g`.

Let's produce a single sed command:

`sed -i -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/{/sha512/! s/=\(.*\)$/=\1+sha512/}' -e '/^[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*\s*=/{s/\b\(sha1\|rmd160\|sha256\|whirlpool\|tiger\|haval\|gost\|crc32\)\b//g}' /etc/aide.conf`

But we need to escape correctly. Using `\b` may be misinterpreted. Use `\<` `\>`: `s/<\(sha1\|... \)>//g`. But need to ensure pattern matching of tokens separated by `+`. In config, tokens have maybe no spaces; they are separated by plus signs. Eg `FIPSR+sha512`. So tokens are joined by `+`. So word boundaries might not properly match because plus signs are not word characters. So we can use pattern `\(sha1\|rmd160\|sha256\|whirlpool\|tiger\|haval\|gost\|crc32\)` preceded by either `+` or start after `=`. But easier: just remove any occurrence of those words. So `s/\b\(sha1\|... \)\b//g` may work.
